author: @kadmow
text: Motion control  - backlash and inertia - 3 issues  - the entire motion space (Ro-Lin, derived from Planar  RoRo  - it differs from SCARA in that the linear motion is contrained, remove that constraint and any "motion profile" mapping is achievable)  - motion paths computed offline OR recorded in a lookup table - point to point is simpler to do the motion control math in CoreXY (linear directions and relative accelerations are) just vector math.  (RIP)

author: @jonathancard4466
text: The control mechanism for this would be really complicated. A print head doesn't just have to move to a specific point; it ha to move along a specific path. To do that, you aren't just controlling the number of rotations of the fee, but the instantaneous speed of the motors. In a current system, you can just scale the head speed but the x- or y-component of the derivative of the path, regardless of the location of the head. This system would require different adjustments depending on the position of the head, because at a constant rotation speed, the head moves along that straight path at different speeds. Large radii=slower linear speed because the linear speed is the sin of the angle of the gear. Which is all calculable. But I'll bet the error would change based on the radius from the center of the print bed. The current systems would have consistent error, at least, everywhere on the print bed. (Putting aside that the more complex speed changes would probably put a lot of strain on the system, somehow.)

author: @dekutree64
text: I think Peopoly has the best answer to this question: linear motors. The printer frame will still have thermal expansion, but significantly less than CoreXY since the long belt path multiplies the expansion effect by 4. And if you make the frame out of steel or carbon fiber it will be less than a typical aluminum frame.

author: @dekutree64
text: Even simpler than two Goodman mechanisms is a SCARA arm. Reprap Morgan is a great design that keeps both motors off the arm without the use of belts. It usually does use belts to increase the resolution and torque, but could use gears, or even direct-drive if your motors have enough positioning accuracy (perhaps e-bike hub motors with high pole count?)

author: @GavinImpett
text:
Some years back I was walked up to a carbon "dust" printer, similar to resin, I suppose, that could "grow" a functional bicycle. Their solution was a metal rail system similar to the risers on the core xy, meaning the head moved on rotating rails. It's significantly more expensive to build.

That said, just tossed my Elegoo and five rolls. Turns out the material won't work with the new company's printer. Having gone through three printer DOAs, based on my talks with Amazon support, 3d printer companies are starting to take quality control seriously if they don't want to get the boot from Amazon, which I suspect is why the nice folks at Flash Forge and Elegoo are reaching out. You can fix these machines without realizing you've done it, not everyone can do that.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @derekfinch9586
text: As always, such enthusiasm! Thanks for all you post. I have worked a lot with robotics and involute gears. I suspect that the gear backlash, that is then multiplied by the arm length, may be worse than the stretch of the core XY belts. Also, if the coefficient of expansion of the (admittedly composite) belt structure were known, even if not linear, the migration from true position could be factored into the slicing software since the working temperature is known (or could be measured with a sensor). This might reduce the belt stretch error by a factor of ten. However, exploring alternative methods of describing any point on an X-Y plane for 3D printing is a highly worthwhile pursuit. Cheers.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Cheers mate - but I do wonder why everyone deals with backlash as if there is nothing you can do about - I know of 5 methods of reducing or eliminating it  - and you are spot on software is one of them lol

replies.author: @derekfinch9586
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I thought you had misunderstood my comment about software but then I realised that software could be used in a gear system to overcome the effect of backlash. Excellent! Thank you.

text: It seems to me that having even a "check point" type of high-resolution feedback can measure the target point, really, really accurately, and do real-time corrections for any changes in the drive system... It seems, even, that discontinuous feedback, combined with temperature and torque sensors, could provide quite good predictive corrections, that are confirmed every time that the head is driven to, or past, a check point... But, hey, I'm a simple test and measurement guy.

author: @timmyssweet
text:
No, I don't agree with that Robert, but I love the experimentation, and it will have a use for something.  

For finer detail, I believe smaller motors and belt would be the answer.  The other adds complexity and potential chatter.  For 1/10 the size, accuracy should increase by a half, at least - in my opinion.  

Secondly, what do you want such fine detail for anyway.  Gears, yes, but they really need to be made from metal.  Fine plastic parts are only really for decoration, and I never thought you were the sort for porcelain doles.  Perhaps, ceramics might need fine detail.  That I am interested in - like belt buckles and watch cases etc.  I've been working on a wrist brace, and small parts that are strong would be really helpful.  However, ceramics and some metals use sintering, so their detail should increase when shrunk.  

Just my thoughts ... I started stating fine detail is useless and found a case for it, ha.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - I see you did mate - Myself i think fine detail can work at the large scale too - better surface means better tolerances even to large gear - well in my mind

author: @mfree80286
text:
Personal theory is the absolute simplest possible accurate 3D printing mechanism would be a rotating table with a print head on a single vertical rod for Z and a worm geared sector with the motor on a second vertical rod to get an arc that covers the table... and let the controller do the math for converting from XY for positioning. 
 As long as you can reach every point on the table and have Z control, you've got something workable.

author: @anathaetownsend1894
text: One issue with gears is that you have to have a gap between the gears in order to allow smooth movement.  This results in backlash.  You would have to add something to remove the backlash, or always move in one direction.

author: @chris993361
text: That's a cool mechanism. I don't think it's a practical replacement for the belts however as you'll be trading some of the issues you're fixing for tolerancing between gears as well as the fact that their linkages and what not change length with varying temperature the same way the belts will. I think it's neat from a maintenance standpoint and just in general however. Thank you for sharing!

author: @owenminor
text: The distance of the motion securing point to the print nozzle is really critical in the design. Unless it's really close, harmonics and wobble are just too much without lots of material and weight. Weight that negates the original intent.

author: @owenminor
text: There were many tests of this in the beginnings of rep-rap. Findings resulted in the mass of the structure to avoid deflection was too expensive or too heavy to make it practical. That means the mass to accelerate was too high. The rotational systems have damping/harmonics that are just too much to implement practically.

author: @LoganKearsley
text: Flex in that long arm, and the multiplication of gear backlash, will ruin the precision you're trying to get. You'd be better off just replacing the belts with lead screws, or keep the turntable to mount a single lead screw mechanism. Transitioning from a Cartesian to a polar coordinate system in either case will, however, result in non-uniform mechanical resolution across the print plane, with better resolution near the center than at the edges.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i like the lead screw idea

replies.author: @hanelyp1
replies.text: The point of coreXY is moving the motor outside the mass being moved.  You could have a worm drive a leadscrew on the X carriage.

replies.author: @LoganKearsley
replies.text: ​@hanelyp1There are definitely ways to accomplish it with either Cartesian lead screws or a turntable. I'd probably go with a spline shaft rather than a worm gear, but I haven't given a ton of thought to it. With a turntable, you can drive the radial screw via a bevel gear with a stationary motor at the axis.

author: @1992jamo
text:
Very very interesting! This could potentially solve the downsides of both delta 3D printers, cartesian, and  core xy. Detla printers excel at drawing circles and curves, but have mildly less accuracy in drawing straight lines, while cartesian and core xy have slightly less accuracy when drawing curves or circles.
edit: Awesome stuff with the version without the ring gear.
